Now that we have matched features, we can work out how the **camera moved** between the two frames — how it **rotated** and which way it **translated**. (We assume the camera is *calibrated*: its intrinsics **K** are known, which is what lets us recover real motion rather than just a distorted version of it.)

The trick is **epipolar geometry**. A feature seen in one image doesn't reveal its depth — it could lie anywhere along the ray from the camera through that pixel. But seen from the *other* camera, that whole ray collapses to a single **line**, so the matching feature must lie somewhere on it. The 3D scene below shows the setup: the same camera at two positions, **A** ($F_{t-1}$) and **B** ($F_t$), each imaging a few points. Drag to orbit, and move camera B with the sliders.

```{=html}
<iframe src="pages/html/epipolar_3d.html" width="100%" height="676" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" style="display:block;border:0;"></iframe>
```

The two things we want — the rotation and the translation between A and B — are bundled into one 3×3 matrix, the **essential matrix E**:

$$
E = [\,t\,]_\times \, R
$$

Here **R** is the **rotation** between the two cameras, and $[\,t\,]_\times$ is the **translation** $t$ written as a matrix:

$$
[\,t\,]_\times =
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & -t_z & t_y \\ t_z & 0 & -t_x \\ -t_y & t_x & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

In the 3D scene that's exactly it: **R** is the difference in the two cameras' orientation, and **t** is the **baseline** — the dashed line from A to B. So E is just a compact way of writing *where B sits relative to A*.

#### Finding E from the matches

We never measure R and t directly — all we have are the **×'s**: the pixel where each camera sees the same feature. A match gives a point $x$ in frame A and its partner $x'$ in frame B, and every correct match obeys one simple relation:

$$
x'^{\top}\, E \, x = 0
$$

In words: $x'$ must lie on the **epipolar line** of $x$ — the line from the visualization. Each match gives one such equation, so with about **eight matches** there are enough equations to solve for E. Once E is known, it **splits back into R and t** — the camera's motion, which we then chain together to track the camera and hand to triangulation for the 3D map.

One catch: from a single camera the *length* of $t$ can't be recovered, only its direction — so the trajectory and map come out correct in shape but at an arbitrary overall **scale**.

```{=html}
<!-- Interactive epipolar-estimation explorer (hidden for now; source kept at pages/html/epipolar.html)
<iframe src="pages/html/epipolar.html" width="100%" height="720px" frameborder="0" scrolling="no"></iframe>
-->
```
